<findings>

[observation] [info] Isolated files — no import edges between 6 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
[observation] [info] Two execution log files (.rex/execution-log.jsonl and .rex/execution-log.1.jsonl) suggest manual rotation has occurred; automating log rotation with a configurable max-file-count would reduce manual maintenance.
[observation] [info] archive.json (max 100 batches, auto-trimmed) has a well-defined retention policy — a good model to apply to execution logs as well.
[observation] [info] prd.json is a single-file PRD store read by both the web server (in-process cache) and CLI commands; atomic write semantics (write-to-temp then rename) should be verified in the rex store implementation to prevent partial-read corruption.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "PRD Runtime State" (prd-runtime-state) has at-risk risk (score: 0.50, cohesion: 0.00, coupling: 0.00) — approaching architectural risk thresholds

</findings>

<insights>

- Isolated files — no import edges between 6 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
- These files are the shared mutable state across all n-dx commands; any command that writes here is a potential concurrency conflict point.
- Zero cohesion and coupling is expected — these are pure data artifacts, not source modules, so import-graph analysis produces no edges.
- Execution logs (.rex/execution-log.jsonl) growing unbounded could become a performance concern; a rotation or max-size policy would improve long-running project hygiene.
- prd.json is a single-file PRD store read by both the web server (in-process cache) and CLI commands; atomic write semantics (write-to-temp then rename) should be verified in the rex store implementation to prevent partial-read corruption.
- Two execution log files (.rex/execution-log.jsonl and .rex/execution-log.1.jsonl) suggest manual rotation has occurred; automating log rotation with a configurable max-file-count would reduce manual maintenance.
- archive.json (max 100 batches, auto-trimmed) has a well-defined retention policy — a good model to apply to execution logs as well.

</insights>
